mappings.rationaleAI: Buddhism as a comprehensive article covering Four Noble Truths, Noble Eightfold Path, dependent arising, karma, three marks of existence, nirvana, samsara, Theravada/Mahayana/Vajrayana schools, and historical spread addresses the entire scope: history (A), intellectual expressions (B), and beliefs, practices, and institutions (C).

Mahayana as the largest Buddhist branch with its bodhisattva ideal, Prajnaparamita, Madhyamaka, Yogacara, and Buddha-nature doctrines directly addresses the Mahayana version (C.2.b). Its spread to China (A.4) and East Asia (A.5) are covered in the historical sections.

Theravada as Buddhism's oldest school with Pali Tipitaka, Abhidhamma philosophy, vipassana meditation, and forest tradition directly addresses the Theravada school (C.2.a). Its dominance in Sri Lanka and Southeast Asia connects to Buddhism in Ceylon and Southeast Asia (A.7).

Siddhartha Gautama's attainment of enlightenment, the Four Noble Truths, Noble Eightfold Path, and establishment of the monastic sangha directly address the founding of Buddhism (A.2). His teachings constitute traditional beliefs and doctrines (C.1). Doctrines concerning Buddha and Buddhahood are in C.2.a.iii.

Hinduism as the cultural context and background from which Buddhism emerged, sharing concepts of dharma, karma, samsara, and moksha, directly addresses the cultural context and its background in Hinduism (A.1).
The Indus Valley Civilisation as part of the broader South Asian cultural context from which Buddhism emerged relates to the geographic and cultural base of Buddhism (A.1).
The Silk Road's transmission of Buddhism across Central Asia and into China directly addresses Buddhism in Central Asia and China (A.4).
The Tang dynasty's Confucian-Buddhist-Daoist religious synthesis and Buddhist transmission to East Asia connect to Buddhism in China (A.4) and Buddhism in Korea and Japan (A.5).
East Asia as the region where Buddhism spread to China, Korea, and Japan addresses Buddhism in Central Asia and China (A.4) and Buddhism in Korea and Japan (A.5).
Thailand as a predominantly Theravada Buddhist nation directly relates to Buddhism in Southeast Asia (A.7).
Myanmar as a predominantly Theravada Buddhist nation directly relates to Buddhism in Southeast Asia (A.7).
The Himalayas with their Buddhist sacred sites and Tibetan cultural connections directly relate to Buddhism in Tibet and the Himalayan kingdoms (A.6).
Eastern philosophy's Buddhist philosophical traditions including Zen and Indian logic connect to Buddhist philosophy (B.3).
Buddhist doctrine's rejection of a permanent self (anatman) while accepting continuity in rebirth directly addresses views of the nature of reality, specifically the absence of self (C.1.a).
Buddhist conceptions of nirvana and rebirth cycles relate to the goal of the Eightfold Path: Nirvana (C.1.c).
Meditation techniques including samatha and vipassana directly address Buddhist mysticism (B.4) and the emphasis on self-cultivation through levels of meditation (C.2.a.ii).
Ritual types including rites of passage and calendrical rites connect to Buddhist practices and institutions (C.3) and ceremonies and festivals (C.3.c).
Spirituality as engagement with transcendent meaning, including mindfulness and soteriological transformation, connects to Buddhist mysticism (B.4).
Festivals rooted in religious observance connect to Buddhist ceremonies and festivals, the religious year, and popular traditions (C.3.c).
Art's cultural and religious functions broadly connect to the religious and cultural role of Buddhist art (C.4).
Visual arts including sculpture and painting relate to Buddhist symbolism and iconography (C.4.a) and religious expression in the arts (C.4.b).
Painting spanning East Asian traditions with religious iconography connects to religious expression in the arts of Buddhism (C.4.b).
The Gupta Empire's religious tolerance and patronage during a period of Hindu-Buddhist coexistence connect to developments in India during the mid-1st millennium CE (A.3).
The Bhagavad Gita as a Hindu scripture synthesizing dharma and karma provides background on the Hindu context from which Buddhism emerged (A.1).
